SUMMARY:
Neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) are a diverse group of tropical infections that are common in low-income populations in developing regions of Africa, Asia,

## Contents

# Neglected tropical diseases

| Neglected tropical diseases | |
| --- | --- |
|  | |
| Number of people requiring interventions against neglected tropical diseases in 2015[[1]](#cite_note-1) | |
| [Specialty](/wiki/Medical_specialty "Medical specialty") | [Infectious disease](/wiki/Infectious_disease_(medical_speciality) "Infectious disease (medical speciality)") |

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Number_of_people_requiring_interventions_against_neglected_tropical_diseases_%28NTDs%29%2C_OWID.svg/500px-Number_of_people_requiring_interventions_against_neglected_tropical_diseases_%28NTDs%29%2C_OWID.svg.png)

**Neglected tropical diseases** (**NTDs**) are a diverse group of [tropical infections](/wiki/Tropical_infections "Tropical infections") that are common in low-income populations in [developing regions](/wiki/Developing_regions "Developing regions") of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"), [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), and the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas").[[2]](#cite_note-2) They are caused by a variety of [pathogens](/wiki/Pathogen "Pathogen"), such as [viruses](/wiki/Virus "Virus"), [bacteria](/wiki/Bacteria "Bacteria"), [protozoa](/wiki/Protozoa "Protozoa"), and parasitic worms ([helminths](/wiki/Helminths "Helminths")). These diseases are contrasted with the "big three" infectious diseases ([HIV/AIDS](/wiki/HIV/AIDS "HIV/AIDS"), [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis"), and [malaria](/wiki/Malaria "Malaria")), which generally receive greater treatment and research funding.[[3]](#cite_note-Hotez-3) In sub-Saharan Africa, [the effect of](/wiki/Disease_burden "Disease burden") neglected tropical diseases as a group is comparable to that of malaria and tuberculosis.[[4]](#cite_note-4) NTD co-infection can also make HIV/AIDS and tuberculosis more deadly.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Some treatments for NTDs are relatively inexpensive. For example, [praziquantel](/wiki/Praziquantel "Praziquantel") for [schistosomiasis](/wiki/Schistosomiasis "Schistosomiasis") costs about US $0.20 per child per year.[[6]](#cite_note-6) Nevertheless, in 2010 it was estimated that control of neglected diseases would require funding of between US$2 billion and $3 billion over the subsequent five to seven years.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Some pharmaceutical companies have committed to donating all the drug [therapies](/wiki/Therapies "Therapies") required, and [mass drug administration](/wiki/Mass_drug_administration "Mass drug administration") efforts (for example, [mass deworming](/wiki/Mass_deworming "Mass deworming")) have been successful in several countries.[[8]](#cite_note-8) While preventive measures are often more accessible in the [developed world](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country"), they are not universally available in poorer areas.[[9]](#cite_note-Hotez_09-9)

Within developed countries, neglected tropical diseases affect the very poorest in society. In developed countries, the burdens of neglected tropical diseases are often overshadowed by other public health issues. However, many of the same issues put populations at risk in developed as well as developing nations. For example, other problems stemming from poverty, such as lack of adequate [housing](/wiki/Housing "Housing"), can expose individuals to the vectors of these diseases.[[10]](#cite_note-:5-10)

Twenty neglected tropical diseases are prioritized by the [World Health Organization](/wiki/World_Health_Organization "World Health Organization") (WHO), though other organizations define NTDs differently. [Chromoblastomycosis](/wiki/Chromoblastomycosis "Chromoblastomycosis") and other deep [mycoses](/wiki/Mycoses "Mycoses"), [scabies](/wiki/Scabies "Scabies") and other [ectoparasites](/wiki/Ectoparasites "Ectoparasites"), and [snakebite](/wiki/Snakebite "Snakebite") envenomation were added to the WHO list in 2017.[[11]](#cite_note-cntd_WHO_team-11) These diseases are [common](/wiki/Endemic_(epidemiology) "Endemic (epidemiology)") in 149 countries, affecting more than 1.4 billion people (including more than 500 million children)[[12]](#cite_note-12) and costing developing economies billions of dollars every year.[[13]](#cite_note-who.int-13) They resulted in 142,000 deaths in 2013, down from 204,000 deaths in 1990.[[14]](#cite_note-GBD204-14)

## Reasons for neglect

The importance of neglected tropical diseases has been underestimated since many are [asymptomatic](/wiki/Asymptomatic "Asymptomatic") and have long [incubation periods](/wiki/Incubation_period "Incubation period"). The connection between death and a neglected tropical disease that has been latent for a long period is often not realized.[[15]](#cite_note-Fenwick-15) Areas of high [endemicity](/wiki/Endemicity "Endemicity") are often geographically isolated, making treatment and prevention much more difficult.[[16]](#cite_note-:10-16)

There are three other major reasons that these diseases have been overlooked: they mainly affect the poorest countries of the [developing world](/wiki/Developing_world "Developing world"); in recent years public health efforts have focused heavily on decreasing the prevalence of HIV/AIDS, tuberculosis, and malaria[[17]](#cite_note-Feasey-17) (far more resources are given to those three diseases because of their higher mortality rates and higher public awareness of them); and neglected tropical diseases do not currently have a prominent cultural figure to champion their elimination.[[16]](#cite_note-:10-16)[[18]](#cite_note-18)

### Stigma

Neglected tropical diseases are often associated with [social stigma](/wiki/Social_stigma "Social stigma"), making their treatment more complex. Public health research has only recently begun to focus on stigma as a component of the issue. From the 1960s onward, approximately one citation a year related to social stigma. In 2006, there were 458.[[19]](#cite_note-Weiss-19)

Stigma greatly affects disease control by decreasing help-seeking and treatment adherence.[[19]](#cite_note-Weiss-19) Disease control programs since the 1980s have begun to integrate stigma mitigation into their offerings. In [India](/wiki/India "India"), a [leprosy](/wiki/Leprosy "Leprosy") program prioritized the message that "leprosy is curable, not hereditary" in order to inspire optimism in highly affected communities. The goal was to make leprosy a disease "like any other", so as to reduce stigma. At the same time, medical resources were optimized to fulfill the promise that the disease could be cured.[[19]](#cite_note-Weiss-19)

### Economic incentives

Treatment and prevention of neglected tropical diseases are not seen as profitable, so [patents](/wiki/Patent "Patent") and profit play a reduced role in stimulating innovation compared to other diseases. Like all non-commercial areas, communities affected by these diseases are reliant on governments and philanthropy.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Currently, the pharmaceutical industry views [research and development](/wiki/Neglected_tropical_disease_research_and_development "Neglected tropical disease research and development") as highly risky. For this reason, resources are not often put into the field of NTDs, and new chemical products are often expensive. A review of public and private initiatives found that of the 1,393 new chemical products that were marketed between 1975 and 1999, only 16 were related to tropical diseases or [tuberculosis](/wiki/Tuberculosis "Tuberculosis"). The same review found that there was a 13-fold greater chance of a newly marketed drug being for [central nervous system disorders](/wiki/Central_nervous_system_disorders "Central nervous system disorders") or [cancer](/wiki/Cancer "Cancer") than for an NTD.[[21]](#cite_note-Olliaro_Lancet-21)

Because of a lack of economic incentives for the pharmaceutical industry, successful NTD treatment programs have often relied on donations. For instance, the Mectizan Donation Program has donated over 1.8 billion tablets of [ivermectin](/wiki/Ivermectin "Ivermectin").[[22]](#cite_note-22) While [developed countries](/wiki/Developed_country "Developed country") often rely on government-run and private partnerships to fund such projects, [developing nations](/wiki/Developing_country "Developing country") frequently have significantly lower per-person spending on these diseases.[[21]](#cite_note-Olliaro_Lancet-21)

A 2006 report found that the [Gates Foundation](/wiki/Gates_Foundation "Gates Foundation") funded most extra activities to counter these diseases.[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)

### Neglected diseases in developed nations

Since 2008, the concept of "neglected diseases of poverty" has been developed and explored.[[25]](#cite_note-Hotez_14-25) This group of diseases, which overlaps with neglected tropical diseases, also pose a threat to human health in developed nations. In the [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States") alone, there are at least 12 million people with neglected parasitic infections.[[25]](#cite_note-Hotez_14-25) They make up a hidden disease burden among the [poorest people in wealthy societies](/wiki/Social_inequality "Social inequality").[[9]](#cite_note-Hotez_09-9) In developed nations, lack of knowledge in the healthcare industry and lack of conclusive [diagnostic tests](/wiki/Medical_test "Medical test") perpetuate the neglect of this group of diseases.[[26]](#cite_note-:1-26)

In the United States, rates of parasitic infection can be distributed along geographic, racial, and socio-economic lines. Among [African Americans](/wiki/African_Americans "African Americans"), there may be up to 2.8 million cases of [toxocariasis](/wiki/Toxocariasis "Toxocariasis"). Toxocariasis, [trichomoniasis](/wiki/Trichomoniasis "Trichomoniasis"), and some other neglected infections occur in the United States at the same rate as in [Nigeria](/wiki/Nigeria "Nigeria").[[9]](#cite_note-Hotez_09-9) Within the [Hispanic](/wiki/Hispanic "Hispanic") community, neglected infections are concentrated near the [US–Mexico border](/wiki/Mexico%E2%80%93United_States_border "Mexico–United States border"). Vector-borne illnesses are especially high, with some rates approaching those of [Latin America](/wiki/Latin_America "Latin America"). [Chagas disease](/wiki/Chagas_disease "Chagas disease") was found in the US as early as the 1970s.[[27]](#cite_note-27) However, in the developed world, diseases that are associated with poverty are often not addressed comprehensively. This may be due to a lack of economic incentives and public policy failings. A lack of awareness prevents effective policy generation and leaves healthcare services unequipped to address the issue. Additionally, little effort is put into creating and maintaining large data sets on neglected diseases in the United States and other developed nations. The first summit on the issue was held by the Adler Institute on Social Exclusion in the United States in 2009.[[9]](#cite_note-Hotez_09-9)

In [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), a similar trend is seen. Neglected tropical diseases are concentrated in [eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and [southern Europe](/wiki/Southern_Europe "Southern Europe"), where poverty levels are highest. The most prevalent diseases in this region are [ascariasis](/wiki/Ascariasis "Ascariasis"), [trichuriasis](/wiki/Trichuriasis "Trichuriasis"), zoonotic [helminth infections](/wiki/Helminthiasis "Helminthiasis"), and [visceral leishmaniasis](/wiki/Visceral_leishmaniasis "Visceral leishmaniasis"). Migration paths to Europe, most notably to [Spain](/wiki/Spain "Spain"), have brought diseases to Europe as well. As many as 6,000 cases of Chagas disease have been introduced in this way. In response to a growing awareness of the burden on these populations, the [European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control](/wiki/European_Centre_for_Disease_Prevention_and_Control "European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control") has laid out ten public health guidelines. They cover a variety of topics, from [health education](/wiki/Health_education "Health education") and promotion to community partnerships and the development of a minority healthcare workforce.[[9]](#cite_note-Hotez_09-9)

## List of diseases

There is some debate among the WHO, [CDC](/wiki/Centers_for_Disease_Control_and_Prevention "Centers for Disease Control and Prevention"), and [infectious disease](/wiki/Infectious_disease "Infectious disease") experts over which diseases are classified as neglected tropical diseases. Feasey, a researcher in neglected tropical diseases, notes 13 neglected tropical diseases: [ascariasis](/wiki/Ascariasis "Ascariasis"), [Buruli ulcer](/wiki/Buruli_ulcer "Buruli ulcer"), [Chagas disease](/wiki/Chagas_disease "Chagas disease"), [dracunculiasis](/wiki/Dracunculiasis "Dracunculiasis"), [hookworm](/wiki/Hookworm "Hookworm") infection, human [African trypanosomiasis](/wiki/African_trypanosomiasis "African trypanosomiasis"), [leishmaniasis](/wiki/Leishmaniasis "Leishmaniasis"), [leprosy](/wiki/Leprosy "Leprosy"), [lymphatic filariasis](/wiki/Lymphatic_filariasis "Lymphatic filariasis"), [onchocerciasis](/wiki/Onchocerciasis "Onchocerciasis"), [schistosomiasis](/wiki/Schistosomiasis "Schistosomiasis"), [trachoma](/wiki/Trachoma "Trachoma"), and [trichuriasis](/wiki/Trichuriasis "Trichuriasis").[[17]](#cite_note-Feasey-17) Fenwick recognizes 12 "core" neglected tropical diseases: the same as above, excluding hookworm.[[15]](#cite_note-Fenwick-15)

These diseases result from four classes of causative [pathogens](/wiki/Pathogens "Pathogens"): (i) protozoa (Chagas disease, human African trypanosomiasis, and leishmaniasis); (ii) bacteria (Buruli ulcer, leprosy, trachoma, and [yaws](/wiki/Yaws "Yaws")), (iii) helminths or [metazoan](/wiki/Animal "Animal") worms ([cysticercosis](/wiki/Cysticercosis "Cysticercosis")/[taeniasis](/wiki/Taeniasis "Taeniasis"), dracunculiasis, [echinococcosis](/wiki/Echinococcosis "Echinococcosis"), [foodborne trematodiases](/wiki/Helminthiasis "Helminthiasis"), lymphatic filariasis, onchocerciasis, schistosomiasis, and [soil-transmitted helminthiasis](/wiki/Soil-transmitted_helminthiasis "Soil-transmitted helminthiasis")); and (iv) viruses ([dengue](/wiki/Dengue_fever "Dengue fever"), [chikungunya](/wiki/Chikungunya "Chikungunya"), and [rabies](/wiki/Rabies "Rabies")).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The WHO recognizes the twenty diseases below as neglected tropical diseases.[[11]](#cite_note-cntd_WHO_team-11)

### Buruli ulcer

[Buruli ulcer](/wiki/Buruli_ulcer "Buruli ulcer") is caused by the bacterium *[Mycobacterium ulcerans](/wiki/Mycobacterium_ulcerans "Mycobacterium ulcerans")*.[[33]](#cite_note-:2-33) It is related to the bacteria that cause tuberculosis and leprosy. *Mycobacterium ulcerans* produces a toxin, [mycolactone](/wiki/Mycolactone "Mycolactone"), that destroys tissue.[[33]](#cite_note-:2-33) The prevalence of Buruli ulcer is unknown.[[17]](#cite_note-Feasey-17) The risk of mortality is low, although secondary infections can be lethal.[[34]](#cite_note-WHOburuliulcer-34) Morbidity takes the form of deformity, disability, and skin lesions, which can be prevented through early treatment with antibiotics and surgery.[[34]](#cite_note-WHOburuliulcer-34) It is found in Africa, Asia, Australia,[[35]](#cite_note-35) and Latin America.[[36]](#cite_note-BuruliUlcerEndemicCountries-36)

### Chagas disease

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7a/Chagoma.jpg/250px-Chagoma.jpg)

[Chagas disease](/wiki/Chagas_disease "Chagas disease") is also known as American trypanosomiasis. There are approximately 15 million people infected with Chagas disease.[[17]](#cite_note-Feasey-17) Morbidity rates are higher for [immunocompromised](/wiki/Immunodeficiency "Immunodeficiency") individuals, children, and the elderly, but can be very low if the disease is treated early.[[37]](#cite_note-WHOChagasDisease-37) Chagas disease does not kill victims rapidly, instead causing years of debilitating chronic symptoms.

It is caused by the [vector-borne](/wiki/Vector-borne "Vector-borne")[[38]](#cite_note-WorldHealthDay2014-38) protozoa *[Trypanosoma cruzi](/wiki/Trypanosoma_cruzi "Trypanosoma cruzi")*.[[37]](#cite_note-WHOChagasDisease-37) It is spread by contact with *Trypanosoma cruzi*-infected feces of the [triatomine](/wiki/Triatominae "Triatominae") ([assassin bug](/wiki/Assassin_bug "Assassin bug")). The protozoan can enter the body via the bug's bite, skin breaks, or [mucous membranes](/wiki/Mucous_membrane "Mucous membrane"). Infection can result from eating infected food or coming into contact with contaminated bodily fluids.[[37]](#cite_note-WHOChagasDisease-37)

There are two phases of Chagas disease. The acute phase is usually asymptomatic. The first symptoms are usually skin [chancres](/wiki/Chancre "Chancre"), unilateral purplish orbital [oedema](/wiki/Oedema "Oedema"), local [lymphadenopathy](/wiki/Lymphadenopathy "Lymphadenopathy"), and [fever](/wiki/Fever "Fever"), accompanied by a variety of other symptoms depending on the infection site.[[37]](#cite_note-WHOChagasDisease-37) The chronic phase occurs in 30 percent of all infections[[17]](#cite_note-Feasey-17) and can take three forms: asymptomatic (most prevalent), cardiac, and digestive lesions.[[37]](#cite_note-WHOChagasDisease-37)

Chagas disease can be prevented by avoiding insect bites through insecticide spraying, home improvement, bed nets, hygienic food, medical care, laboratory practices, and testing.[[37]](#cite_note-WHOChagasDisease-37) It can be diagnosed through a [serological](/wiki/Serological "Serological") test, although the test is not very accurate.[[17]](#cite_note-Feasey-17) Treatment is with medication, which may have severe side effects.[[37]](#cite_note-WHOChagasDisease-37)

### Dengue and chikungunya

There are 50–100 million [dengue virus](/wiki/Dengue_virus "Dengue virus") infections annually.[[39]](#cite_note-WHODengueControl-39) [Dengue fever](/wiki/Dengue_fever "Dengue fever") is usually not fatal, but infection with one of four serotypes can increase later susceptibility to other serotypes, resulting in a potentially fatal disease called severe dengue.[[39]](#cite_note-WHODengueControl-39) Dengue fever is caused by a [flavivirus](/wiki/Flavivirus "Flavivirus") which is spread mostly by the bite of the *[Aedes aegypti](/wiki/Aedes_aegypti "Aedes aegypti")* mosquito.[[39]](#cite_note-WHODengueControl-39) No treatment for either dengue or severe dengue exists beyond [palliative care](/wiki/Palliative_care "Palliative care").[[39]](#cite_note-WHODengueControl-39) The symptoms are high fever and flu-like symptoms.[[39]](#cite_note-WHODengueControl-39) It is found in Asia, Latin America, and Northern Australia.[[39]](#cite_note-WHODengueControl-39)

[Chikungunya](/wiki/Chikungunya "Chikungunya") is an [arboviral](/wiki/Arbovirus "Arbovirus") disease transmitted by *[A. albopictus](/wiki/Aedes_albopictus "Aedes albopictus")* and *A. aegypti* mosquitoes. The virus was first isolated from an outbreak in [Tanzania](/wiki/Tanzania "Tanzania") in 1952.[[40]](#cite_note-:3-40) Chikungunya virus is a member of the genus *[Alphavirus](/wiki/Alphavirus "Alphavirus")* and family *Togaviridae*.[[40]](#cite_note-:3-40) The word "chikungunya" is from the [Makonde language](/wiki/Makonde_language "Makonde language") and means "that which bends up", referring to the effect of debilitating joint pain on the patient.[[40]](#cite_note-:3-40) Symptoms, generally appearing 5–7 days after exposure, can be confused with dengue and include fever, rash, headache, joint pain, and swelling.[[41]](#cite_note-41) The disease mainly occurs in Africa and Asia.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

### Dracunculiasis

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Dracunculus_medinensis_larvae.jpg/250px-Dracunculus_medinensis_larvae.jpg)

[Dracunculiasis](/wiki/Dracunculiasis "Dracunculiasis") is also known as Guinea-worm disease. In 2019, 53 cases were reported across four countries,[[43]](#cite_note-43) a substantial decrease from 3,500,000 cases in 1986.[[44]](#cite_note-WHO_WER_85-19-44) It is not fatal, but can cause months of inactivity.[[45]](#cite_note-WHODracunculiasis-45) It is caused by drinking water contaminated by [water fleas](/wiki/Cladocera "Cladocera") infected with [guinea-worm](/wiki/Guinea-worm "Guinea-worm") larvae.[[45]](#cite_note-WHODracunculiasis-45) Approximately one year after infection, a painful blister forms and one or more worms emerge. Worms can be up to 1 metre long.[[45]](#cite_note-WHODracunculiasis-45)

It is usually treated by World Health Organization volunteers who clean and bandage wounds caused by worms and return daily to pull the worm out a few more inches.[[45]](#cite_note-WHODracunculiasis-45) Dracunculiasis is preventable by water filtration, immediate case identification to prevent spread, health education, and treating ponds with larvicide. An [eradication program](/wiki/Eradication_of_dracunculiasis "Eradication of dracunculiasis") has been able to reduce prevalence.[[45]](#cite_note-WHODracunculiasis-45) As of 2014[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Neglected_tropical_diseases&action=edit), the four endemic countries are [Chad](/wiki/Chad "Chad"), [Ethiopia](/wiki/Ethiopia "Ethiopia"), [Mali](/wiki/Mali "Mali"), and [South Sudan](/wiki/South_Sudan "South Sudan").[[45]](#cite_note-WHODracunculiasis-45)

### Echinococcosis

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Echinococcus_gran_LifeCycle_lg.jpg/250px-Echinococcus_gran_LifeCycle_lg.jpg)

The rate of [echinococcosis](/wiki/Echinococcosis "Echinococcosis") is higher in rural areas, and there are more than one million people infected currently.[[46]](#cite_note-WHOEchinococcosis-46) It is caused by ingesting [parasites](/wiki/Parasites "Parasites") in animal feces.[[47]](#cite_note-WHO_Transmission_of_echinococcosis-47)

There are two versions of the disease: cystic and [alveolar](/wiki/Alveolar_echinococcosis "Alveolar echinococcosis"). Both versions involve an asymptomatic incubation period of several years. In the cystic version, liver cysts cause [abdominal pain](/wiki/Abdominal_pain "Abdominal pain"), [nausea](/wiki/Nausea "Nausea"), and [vomiting](/wiki/Vomiting "Vomiting"), while cysts in the lungs cause [chronic cough](/wiki/Chronic_cough "Chronic cough"), [chest pain](/wiki/Chest_pain "Chest pain"), and [shortness of breath](/wiki/Shortness_of_breath "Shortness of breath"). In alveolar echinococcosis, a p

## 101: What are neglected tropical diseases (NTDs)

01 February 2024

## Share

![Community members in Salima, Malawi welcome four All Party Parlimentarian Group (APPG) on Malaria and NTDs Parliamentarians, as they visit a patients home.](https://unitingntds.cdn.ngo/media/images/Sightsavers_-_Uniting_to_Combat_NTDs-100.width-800.jpg)

© Malumbo Simwaka / Uniting to Combat NTDs/ Sightsavers

## Answers to frequently asked questions (FAQs)

**What are neglected tropical** **diseases (NTDs)?**

Neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) are a group of twenty-one different diseases and infections that can cause significant disfigurement, disability, and death.  For more on each NTD, see our [disease pages](https://unitingtocombatntds.org/en/neglected-tropical-diseases/disease-directory/).

**Who do they affect?**

They affect around 1.65 billion people around the world and tend to thrive in warm climates and in rural, poverty-stricken areas where access to clean water and healthcare is limited.

**How long have NTDs existed?**

NTDs have burdened humanity since the beginning of recorded history. For example, descriptions of NTDs such as trachoma, guinea worm, and others are found in the Bible. Evidence of schistosomiasis infection has been found in Egyptian mummies, the presence of river blindness was documented in African art and folklore, and leprosy was recorded in ancient texts from China and India.

**Why are NTDs considered to be neglected?**

NTDs have earned their name for a reason – these diseases, the people, and the regions they afflict typically have been overlooked, receiving very little global attention and funding for research, prevention, and control efforts. Though one out of every five people worldwide are at risk of NTDs, many people are not aware of the existence of these debilitating diseases.

The term “neglected tropical diseases” emerged in the early 21st century as global health organizations and researchers sought to draw attention to the unique challenges posed by these diseases and advocate for increased efforts to control and eliminate them.

**What causes NTDs?**

NTDs can be transmitted by various microscopic parasites and bacteria that can spread through dirty water, soil, bites from insects like mosquitoes and sandflies, or close contact with other people – often through droplets or secretions through the nose, eyes, or mouth.

**What is it like to have an NTD?**

NTDs wreak havoc on peoples’ lives and can cause immense suffering, chronic disability, disfigurement, and death. Trachoma, for example, is an eye infection that causes painful scarring of the eyelids and blindness.  Leprosy affects the nerves, resulting in muscle weakness, numbness, and deformities, particularly in the hands and feet. Visceral leishmaniasis affects internal organs like the spleen and liver, causing severe anemia, organ failure, and death. For a complete overview of all twenty-one NTDs and their symptoms, please visit the [disease directory](/en/neglected-tropical-diseases/disease-directory/).

**How else do NTDs impact** **affected communities?**

NTDs not only compromise physical health, but also undermine emotional well-being, educational pursuits, and economic stability. Here’s how:

**What progress has been made fighting NTDs?**

Despite limited funding, many countries have made significant progress controlling and treating NTDs. As of July 2024, 51 countries have eliminated at least one NTD, meaning that those diseases no longer pose a threat to people living in those countries. Meanwhile, researchers are developing new treatments and better tools to diagnose and prevent NTDs. Contributing to this success is a group of committed donors, including the pharmaceutical industry, which generously contributed over 15 billion treatments between 2012 and 2022. In 2022 alone, impressively around 820 million people received treatment for NTDs, the result of a monumental effort to reach people in need.

**What are the targets  for NTDs?**

Disease-specific targets for 2030 and milestones for 2023 and 2025 were set for each of the 20 diseases and disease groups for one of the following:

(a) eradication, defined as permanent reduction to zero of the worldwide incidence of a specific pathogen, as a result of deliberate efforts, with no risk of reintroduction;

(b) elimination (interruption of transmission), defined as reduction to zero of the incidence of infection caused by a specific pathogen in a defined geographical area, with minimal risk of reintroduction, as a result of deliberate efforts; continued action to prevent re-establishment of transmission may be required;

(c) elimination as a public health problem, is a term related to both infection and disease, defined by achievement of measurable targets set by WHO in relation to a specific disease. When reached, continued action is required to maintain the targets and/or to advance interruption of transmission;

(d) control, defined as reduction of disease incidence, prevalence, morbidity and/or mortality to a locally acceptable level as a result of deliberate efforts; continued interventions are required to maintain the reduction.

**What is needed to end NTDs?**

Exciting times are ahead in the fight against NTDs. To succeed, we need more global leaders to commit to ending NTDs and pledge crucial funding. Investing in new research is also key, developing more tools that align with community needs. And NTD interventions must be integrated into existing primary healthcare packages for everyone in at-risk areas.

By working together, we can create a future where everyone, no matter where they live, can lead a healthy and happy life. Join the movement – let's make NTDs history!

# About Neglected Tropical Diseases

## At a glance

## Overview

People in affected communities often lack access to basic or adequate resources, like medical care or sanitation. Safe and effective medicines can control many NTDs through mass drug administration (MDA) programs in the affected communities.

CDC supports global programs to eliminate the following NTDs

## [Neglected Tropical Diseases](/neglected-tropical-diseases/index.html)

Neglected tropical diseases (NTDs) include several parasitic viral, bacterial, fungal, and non-communicable diseases that cause substantial illness for more than one billion people globally.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Neglected Tropical Diseases (NTDs) are infections prevalent in tropical regions, impacting over 1.4 billion people, with examples including schistosomiasis, Chagas disease, and leprosy.
NTDs disproportionately affect low-income populations, causing significant morbidity and mortality, with an estimated 142,000 deaths and billions in economic losses annually.
NTDs hinder economic development, reduce productivity, and create a cycle of poverty, affecting education and healthcare access in affected communities.
Key challenges include limited funding, lack of political will, inadequate healthcare infrastructure, and social stigma associated with these diseases.
There is a critical need for global awareness, funding, and integrated healthcare approaches to combat NTDs effectively and improve health outcomes for affected populations.
